mnemonics.title: ACCESSING NESTED MEMBERS
mnemonics.content: DAD (Dot After Dot): When accessing nested members, keep using dot (.) for each level - like going through family generations: grandfather.father.son

mnemonics.title: NESTED INITIALIZATION
mnemonics.content: INNER FIRST, OUTER NEXT: Always initialize from innermost to outermost structure using nested braces: {{inner values}, outer values}

mnemonics.title: POINTER ACCESS RULE
mnemonics.content: ARROW MEANS GO INSIDE: When you have a pointer (->), it's like opening a door to access nested members: pointer->member.nestedMember

acronyms.expansion: Hierarchical relationship where one structure contains another structure as a member
Direct Object Transfer - operator used to access structure members directly
acronyms.usage: emp.name accesses name member of emp structure

visualTips.visualization: Think of nested structures like Russian nesting dolls - each structure fits inside the other, stored one after another in contiguous memory blocks like boxes stacked in a room.
Draw a tree diagram with root as outer structure, branches as nested structures, and leaves as individual members. Trace path from root to leaf to form the access expression.
Use layered brackets: outer { [inner {values}], otherOuter } - like organizing folders inside folders with files inside each.

analogies.analogy: Like a house (outer structure) containing rooms (inner structures), each room having furniture (members). To describe a specific chair, you say house.room.chair.
Like a university department having departments, each having courses, each having students. Nested structures perfectly map this organization: Dept.Course.Student.
It's like having a house address (pointer) that directly points to a specific room inside, so you can access room contents without going through the main house first.
